nodes.label: Evaluator
nodes.description: Decide whether the platform can solve the job

nodes.label: Learner
nodes.description: Build enough understanding to act safely

nodes.label: Adopter
nodes.description: Make the core capability work once

nodes.label: Integrator
nodes.description: Connect the capability to an existing system

nodes.label: Shipper
nodes.description: Make it work in a production-like environment

nodes.label: Operator
Contributor
nodes.description: Change the project and get the change accepted

nodes.label: Partner
nodes.description: Publish an integration, extension, template, or marketplace offering

nodes.label: Problem definition
nodes.description: The first-mile endpoint and population are defined

nodes.label: Before an attempt
nodes.description: The developer has not begun a platform attempt

nodes.label: Discovery and evaluation
nodes.description: The developer is finding and judging a starting path

nodes.label: Access and authority
nodes.description: Accounts, commercial gates, permissions, and organizational approval

nodes.label: Learning and setup
nodes.description: The developer is forming a model and preparing an environment

nodes.label: Implementation
nodes.description: The developer is configuring, building, or integrating

nodes.label: Verification and recovery
nodes.description: The developer is checking a result or recovering from failure

nodes.label: Representative use
nodes.description: The developer is adapting the first result to the real job

nodes.label: Cross-cutting context
nodes.description: Human, team, risk, support, agent, access, or measurement conditions

nodes.label: The first mile is defined incorrectly
The team defines signup as success.
The team defines installation as success.
The team defines a quickstart completion as success even when it does not predict continued use.
The team chooses a milestone because it is easy to instrument.
The milestone reflects the platform's internal architecture instead of the developer's job.
The milestone is the same for evaluators, adopters, integrators, operators, contributors, and partners.
The milestone ignores the developer's existing system.
The milestone uses toy input that hides the real adaptation work.
The milestone stops before the developer can verify the result.
The milestone requires production readiness when the developer only needs an evaluation result.
The milestone is too shallow to create confidence.
The milestone is so ambitious that it combines the first mile with the first marathon.
The platform's completion signal and the developer's success signal diverge.
The team studies a funnel stage without naming the developer, job, or context.
Different teams use different first-mile definitions and compare incompatible data.
No attempt begins
The developer has no urgent problem.
The problem is real but not important enough now.
Another project has higher priority.
The developer is curious but has no implementation mandate.
The developer cannot connect the platform to a current job.
The expected value is smaller than the expected effort.
The developer assumes an incumbent tool is good enough.
The developer expects a build-versus-buy decision later.
The developer fears creating a maintenance burden.
The developer fears personal blame if the experiment fails.
A manager, architect, procurement owner, or security owner has already discouraged the category.
The developer is attending content for education, not adoption.
The call to action arrives at the wrong moment.
The developer saves the resource and forgets it.
The developer lacks uninterrupted time to start.
Discovery and entry fail
The developer cannot find the official starting point.
Search results lead to obsolete docs, forks, blog posts, or cached pages.
Product, documentation, GitHub, console, and marketing pages offer competing starts.
The navigation is organized by internal product names.
The platform has no path for the developer's language, framework, or use case.
The quickstart is buried below conceptual material.
The conceptual overview is missing, so the quickstart has no context.
A QR code, short link, package name, repository, or CLI command is wrong.
Regional domains or corporate filters block the entry page.
The developer lands on an enterprise sales form instead of a trial path.
The developer cannot tell which edition, product, or repository is official.
The developer cannot tell whether to start in a dashboard, CLI, API, SDK, or template.
The developer follows a path intended for administrators or end users.
The platform requires an invitation that has not arrived.
Deep links lose the intended context after authentication.
Relevance, positioning, and trust fail
The value proposition is vague.
The examples do not resemble the developer's job.
The platform appears designed for a different company size, industry, or maturity level.
The platform's terminology does not match the developer's mental model.
Marketing claims are stronger than the available evidence.
The developer cannot distinguish the platform from alternatives.
The developer cannot see the tradeoff the platform is making.
The developer suspects lock-in.
The developer doubts project or vendor longevity.
The developer doubts maintenance quality.
The developer sees unresolved incidents, abandoned issues, or stale releases.
The developer cannot find pricing, limits, security, privacy, or support terms.
The developer sees a mismatch between documentation promises and product behavior.
Community responses appear dismissive, hostile, or sales-driven.
The platform requires trust before it provides inspectable evidence.
Account, identity, and authentication fail
Signup is mandatory before value can be evaluated.
Signup requires a work email the developer cannot or will not use.
Personal emails, aliases, or privacy relays are rejected.
Verification email is delayed, filtered, expired, or never delivered.
SSO is required but not configured.
SSO routes to the wrong identity provider or tenant.
Multi-factor authentication is unavailable or fails.
The developer cannot use the required phone number or authenticator.
OAuth consent is blocked by the organization.
OAuth requests excessive or unexplained scopes.
CAPTCHA is inaccessible, broken, or blocked.
The account already exists under another identity.
Account linking creates duplicate or orphaned accounts.
Session state is lost across docs, console, CLI, and support.
The CLI or SDK authentication flow cannot run in the developer's environment.
Tokens expire during the tutorial without a recovery path.
The developer cannot sign out or switch accounts safely.
The organization prohibits creation of external accounts.
Organization, workspace, and tenancy setup fail
The developer creates a personal workspace when the tutorial assumes an organization.
The developer joins the wrong organization, project, region, or tenant.
The developer lacks permission to create a project or resource.
Only an administrator can accept terms or activate the account.
The organization invitation expires or targets another email identity.
Role names do not explain their practical permissions.
Default roles are too restrictive for the quickstart.
Default roles are too broad for the organization's policy.
The tutorial assumes a single-user workspace.
Resource ownership is assigned to the wrong person or team.
Deleting a test workspace requires privileges the developer lacks.
Region, residency, or tenant selection is irreversible or unclear.
Existing organization policy disables the required feature.
Trial entitlements differ between personal and organization accounts.
The developer cannot see whether a resource is personal, shared, or production-affecting.
Commercial, procurement, and legal gates fail
A credit card is required before evaluation.
The developer cannot use a personal or corporate card.
A free tier is unavailable in the developer's country or use case.
Trial credits are too small, expire too quickly, or are hard to understand.
Pricing units are unfamiliar or impossible to estimate.
The cost of a realistic test is unknown.
The developer fears an unbounded bill.
Budget alerts, caps, or hard limits are missing.
Procurement must approve a vendor before any data is uploaded.
Legal terms prohibit the intended data, geography, or usage.
The developer cannot accept click-through terms for the company.
Open-source license obligations are unclear or unacceptable.
SDK or model licenses conflict with the product's distribution model.
A business associate agreement, DPA, security review, or vendor assessment is required.
Tax, currency, invoicing, or billing-entity constraints block activation.
The platform requires a sales conversation to obtain essential limits or access.
The developer cannot tell before starting whether meaningful use is self-serve, invite-only, sales-assisted, administrator-approved, partner-only, or contract-gated.
The platform requests payment information without explaining whether it is for identity verification, fraud prevention, a temporary hold, prepaid credit, a subscription, or usage billing.
Authority, permissions, and governance fail
The developer cannot install software on the workstation.
The developer cannot run containers, virtual machines, or local services.
The developer cannot create cloud resources.
The developer cannot create service accounts, keys, certificates, or OAuth apps.
The developer cannot connect a source-control organization.
A source-control administrator must approve the app.
The developer cannot change DNS, firewall, network, or identity settings.
The developer cannot use production-like data.
The developer cannot invite collaborators or create a shared project.
A policy engine rejects the configuration without an actionable explanation.
Separation-of-duties rules prevent one person from completing the tutorial.
Audit, retention, encryption, or ownership requirements are not met.
The developer does not know who can grant the missing permission.
Approval takes longer than the developer's evaluation window.
The platform does not distinguish safe sandbox actions from consequential production actions.
Documentation and information architecture fail
Documentation is incomplete.
Documentation is incorrect.
Documentation is stale.
Documentation mixes versions without warning.
Documentation assumes context the developer does not have.
Documentation explains syntax but not the decision being made.
Documentation describes features but not end-to-end jobs.
Required prerequisites appear after the step that needs them.
A critical caveat is hidden in a note, issue, changelog, or FAQ.
Code and prose disagree.
Screenshots no longer match the interface.
Copy buttons omit line breaks, prompts, placeholders, or required context.
Links are broken, redirected, access-controlled, or circular.
Search returns noisy, duplicated, or irrelevant results.
The API reference lacks conceptual guidance.
Conceptual guidance lacks runnable examples.
Error documentation is absent or not searchable by the actual message.
The developer cannot tell whether a step is required, optional, destructive, or production-affecting.
The happy path has no recovery instructions.
Localization is missing, poor, or inconsistent with product terminology.
Learning and cognitive load fail
The developer lacks a prerequisite concept.
The developer knows the domain but not the platform's vocabulary.
The developer must learn several new abstractions at once.
The tutorial introduces choices before giving selection criteria.
Similar concepts have indistinguishable names.
One concept has different names across product surfaces.
The platform exposes implementation detail before the core mental model.
The developer cannot predict the consequence of a configuration choice.
The developer cannot tell which values are examples and which must be replaced.
The developer copies commands without understanding their state changes.
The developer cannot map the toy example to an existing architecture.
The tutorial's pace assumes more expertise than the developer has.
The tutorial is so basic that an experienced developer abandons it.
The platform requires simultaneous knowledge of product, cloud, networking, security, and domain rules.
The developer cannot form a causal model of success or failure.
Prerequisite and local-environment setup fail
The operating system is unsupported.
The CPU architecture is unsupported.
The runtime version is unsupported.
The package manager is missing or incompatible.
Required compilers, headers, build tools, or system libraries are missing.
The required browser or browser feature is unavailable.
The shell differs from the documented shell.
Path, quoting, line-ending, locale, or encoding behavior differs.
The developer lacks disk, memory, CPU, GPU, or battery capacity.
The device clock is wrong and breaks certificates or signatures.
Corporate endpoint protection quarantines a binary or script.
Proxy, VPN, DNS, firewall, or TLS inspection changes behavior.
A required port is occupied or blocked.
The developer is offline or on an unreliable connection.
The environment already contains conflicting tools, variables, certificates, or credentials.
The tutorial assumes administrator or root privileges.
The developer cannot reproduce the tutorial's clean environment.
Remote development, containers, WSL, VDI, or codespaces create an undocumented boundary.
Installation, dependency, build, and versioning fail
The package or binary name is ambiguous.
The package registry is blocked or unavailable.
The package is missing for the target platform.
Checksums, signatures, provenance, or certificates fail.
A dependency is yanked, abandoned, compromised, or incompatible.
Transitive dependency resolution produces a conflict.
Lockfiles and documented versions disagree.
The latest version contains a regression.
The tutorial requires an older version without pinning it.
Global and project-local installations conflict.
Multiple copies of the runtime or CLI resolve unpredictably.
Native compilation fails.
The build exceeds resource or time limits.
A monorepo, workspace, proxy registry, or private mirror changes installation behavior.
The developer cannot remove or roll back the installation cleanly.
An upgrade or migration is required before the first useful action.
Configuration, credentials, secrets, and state fail
A required configuration value is undocumented.
A default is unsafe, surprising, or unsuitable for the tutorial.
A placeholder is mistaken for a literal value.
A literal example value is mistaken for a placeholder.
Environment-variable names differ across docs, SDKs, CLI, and runtime.
Configuration precedence is unclear.
The platform reads configuration from an unexpected directory or account.
A secret is created but cannot be retrieved again.
A key has the wrong scope, environment, region, tenant, or expiration.
Test and production credentials are confused.
Credentials are copied with whitespace, quotes, line breaks, or truncation.
Secret storage is unavailable or prohibited.
The developer accidentally commits a secret and must stop to rotate it.
State from a prior attempt contaminates the current attempt.
State is lost between browser, CLI, local process, and remote runtime.
The developer cannot tell which configuration is active.
Configuration changes are eventually consistent without a visible status.
Cleanup removes state needed for another step.
Network, region, and external-dependency fail
The platform endpoint is blocked by a firewall, proxy, VPN, ISP, or national policy.
DNS fails or resolves differently across environments.
TLS trust, certificate chains, SNI, or interception cause failures.
IPv4 and IPv6 behavior differs.
A callback, tunnel, webhook, or local port cannot receive inbound traffic.
Egress is restricted.
The chosen region is unavailable, distant, or incompatible with another resource.
Cross-region access is prohibited or too slow.
An external provider, registry, identity service, payment rail, or API is down.
The external dependency requires its own account, approval, or payment.
Rate limits, bot protection, WAF rules, or abuse controls block the attempt.
Timeouts are shorter than the operation requires.
Retries duplicate a non-idempotent action.
A webhook sender cannot verify the receiver.
Sandbox endpoints behave differently from production endpoints.
Network failure is reported as a generic authentication or application error.
Data, schema, and input fail
The tutorial's sample data is unavailable.
Sample data is too clean to reveal real constraints.
The developer cannot use representative data because of privacy or policy.
File type, encoding, delimiter, size, shape, or compression is unsupported.
Schema requirements are undocumented or inferred incorrectly.
Required fields are missing.
Field names, types, units, time zones, or null behavior differ.
Test fixtures are stale or inconsistent.
Seed operations partially succeed and leave ambiguous state.
Data import, indexing, training, or processing is asynchronous with no progress signal.
The input exceeds a hidden or poorly explained limit.
The input triggers moderation, validation, fraud, or abuse controls.
The developer cannot safely delete uploaded data.
The output is technically valid but not meaningful for the developer's input.
A data residency or retention rule blocks the intended flow.
Product, API, SDK, CLI, and interface design fail
The primary action is hard to find.
The interface presents too many choices before first value.
Defaults optimize for advanced or production users instead of evaluation.
Required and optional fields are indistinguishable.
Names do not match the docs or domain language.
The same task behaves differently in console, CLI, API, and SDK.
The API requires too many round trips for the first job.
The SDK hides critical behavior or exposes too much low-level detail.
The CLI is not scriptable, inspectable, or non-interactive when needed.
The UI cannot express the configuration shown in code.
The API cannot express the configuration shown in the UI.
Destructive and reversible actions look alike.
The developer cannot preview a change.
The developer cannot inspect generated configuration or requests.
Resource names, identifiers, and relationships are hard to copy or recognize.
Pagination, asynchronous operations, eventual consistency, or idempotency is hidden.
A feature is visible but not enabled for the account.
The first-run tour blocks rather than assists the real task.
Examples, templates, starters, and generated code fail
The example does not run.
The example uses an obsolete API, SDK, dependency, or product feature.
The example contains undeclared setup.
The example requires an unavailable asset, secret, service, or dataset.
The example is too minimal to show adaptation points.
The example is too complex to isolate the core capability.
The example's architecture conflicts with common production patterns.
The template includes insecure or expensive defaults.
Generated code is opaque or difficult to modify.
Generated code compiles but does not produce the promised behavior.
The sample repository has broken branches, submodules, large files, or missing releases.
The developer cannot tell which parts to copy and which parts to understand.
The example only works in the vendor's preferred language or framework.
The example requires replacing many coupled placeholders.
The example succeeds only because it uses privileged vendor-owned resources.
The example has no tests, expected output, or verification step.
Feedback, errors, diagnosis, and recovery fail
The action appears to do nothing.
Progress is invisible.
Success is declared before the outcome is actually available.
Failure is reported after a long delay.
The error is generic.
The error describes an internal implementation detail.
The error blames the wrong layer.
The same root cause produces different messages across surfaces.
The error message is not searchable.
The suggested fix is wrong, unsafe, or incomplete.
Logs are unavailable, delayed, truncated, noisy, or in another product area.
Correlation IDs, timestamps, request IDs, and resource IDs are missing.
The developer cannot distinguish platform failure from application failure.
The developer cannot reproduce the failure.
Retry behavior is unclear.
Partial success leaves resources that change the next attempt.
There is no reset, undo, rollback, or clean restart path.
Cleanup instructions are destructive beyond the tutorial scope.
Support asks the developer to repeat steps already completed.
Recovery requires privileged access or a sales/support intervention.
Reliability, performance, and capacity fail
The service is down.
The control plane is available but the data plane is not.
The dashboard is available but API or CLI operations fail.
Provisioning, build, deployment, indexing, or model loading takes longer than expected.
Cold starts or wake-up delays look like failure.
Capacity is unavailable in the chosen region or tier.
A queue is backed up.
The sandbox is less reliable than production.
Resource limits are lower than documented or expected.
A noisy neighbor or shared runner causes intermittent failure.
Eventual consistency produces contradictory states.
Rate limiting begins before the developer expects it.
The status page does not reflect the affected component.
The developer cannot tell whether waiting or changing something is correct.
The first successful run is not repeatable.
Security, privacy, compliance, and risk fail
The developer cannot determine what data leaves the environment.
The platform requests more data or permissions than the job requires.
Security documentation is missing, stale, vague, or gated.
Encryption, key management, audit, retention, deletion, or residency controls are unclear.
The platform cannot use the organization's identity, network, or secrets model.
The tutorial encourages unsafe secret handling.
The starter exposes a public endpoint or resource unexpectedly.
Dependency or container scanning finds unacceptable vulnerabilities.
The platform, SDK, model, extension, or binary lacks trusted provenance.
The organization prohibits the data category, model, region, or subprocessors.
The developer cannot verify data deletion.
The platform's abuse controls reject legitimate evaluation behavior.
Compliance claims do not map clearly to the developer's application responsibilities.
The developer cannot create a safe sandbox separated from production.
The first-mile path requires disabling a security control.
Cost, quota, limits, and entitlement fail
The required feature is not included in the current plan.
The docs do not identify the required plan.
The free tier excludes the realistic first-mile path.
Trial credits are not applied automatically.
Quotas start at zero or require an increase request.
The developer hits an account, project, region, concurrency, size, rate, or resource limit.
A hidden limit fails the attempt without naming the limit.
Usage meters update too slowly to guide decisions.
Cost estimates omit dependent resources, egress, storage, tokens, seats, or minimums.
Cleanup does not stop billing.
The developer fears testing because the blast radius is unknown.
Credits expire while an approval or support request is pending.
Enterprise entitlements require a contract update.
A marketplace, cloud, or reseller account has different entitlements.
The first-mile path is technically possible but economically irrational.
Team, ownership, and organizational coordination fail
No one owns the evaluation.
Several teams own dependent steps.
The developer can start but not complete the path alone.
The champion leaves, changes priority, or loses sponsorship.
The manager wants evidence the first mile cannot yet provide.
Architecture, platform, security, data, legal, and procurement owners disagree.
The platform solves one team's problem while creating another team's burden.
The developer cannot identify the internal owner of credentials, data, infrastructure, or DNS.
A shared sandbox is contaminated or occupied by another team.
Naming, tagging, ownership, or cost-allocation standards block resource creation.
The tutorial assumes authority that belongs to another function.
Handoffs lose context, logs, or state.
The team's support channel is unavailable across time zones.
The adopter must persuade others before any meaningful test.
Success would require a process change the organization is unwilling to make.
Transition from toy success to representative use fails
The hello world works but the developer cannot locate the adaptation seam.
The developer cannot substitute their own data, repository, account, domain, or workflow.
The real architecture requires multiple services while the example uses one.
The real environment requires private networking, SSO, policy, or compliance controls absent from the example.
The real workload exceeds sandbox limits.
The real job is asynchronous while the example is synchronous.
The real job requires retries, idempotency, ordering, consistency, or reconciliation.
The real job requires observability, testing, rollback, backup, or disaster recovery.
The real job requires collaboration, review, approval, or handoff.
The developer cannot estimate performance, reliability, cost, or operational burden.
The integration conflicts with existing architecture or conventions.
The platform requires a migration or data movement before value is visible.
The developer cannot run the same path in local, test, staging, and production environments.
The developer cannot explain the change to an approver or maintainer.
The first example proves capability but not fitness for the developer's job.
Accessibility, language, geography, and inclusion fail
Keyboard navigation is incomplete.
Focus is lost, hidden, or trapped.
Screen-reader names, roles, states, or ordering are missing.
Color, contrast, zoom, text size, or motion makes the path unusable.
CAPTCHA, diagrams, video, or audio lacks an accessible alternative.
The documentation assumes fluent English.
Translation changes technical meaning.
Dates, numbers, currency, addresses, names, phone numbers, or time zones use unsupported formats.
The platform, feature, payment method, phone verification, or region is unavailable in the developer's country.
The path assumes a powerful laptop, fast network, second screen, or modern phone.
The tutorial assumes visual copying, drag-and-drop, precise pointer use, or sustained attention.
Error and support paths are not localized.
Community participation feels unsafe or exclusionary.
Documentation examples encode cultural assumptions that obscure the job.
Time-zone and event timing prevent access to live help.
Support, community, and escalation fail
No support channel is visible.
The visible channel is for sales, not technical help.
Support requires a paid plan.
Community support requires another account or public disclosure.
The developer cannot share proprietary code or data needed to reproduce the issue.
The issue template requests unavailable information.
The support response is too slow for the evaluation window.
The support response assumes the developer is a beginner or an expert incorrectly.
The response links back to the same failed docs.
The community provides several contradictory fixes.
Maintainers cannot reproduce the environment.
The issue is closed as configuration, duplicate, unsupported, or stale without a usable next step.
The developer fears embarrassment or reputational cost from asking.
The developer does not know whether the problem belongs to the platform, integration, dependency, or their code.
Escalation requires an account ID, contract, or admin the developer cannot access.
Time, attention, motivation, and emotion fail
The path takes longer than the available session.
The developer is interrupted and cannot resume.
State expires between attempts.
The developer is fatigued by prior setup work.
Too many small uncertainties accumulate into abandonment.
The developer interprets friction as a signal of future platform quality.
The developer is afraid of breaking production or incurring cost.
The developer feels foolish for not understanding the path.
The developer has already spent political capital on the tool.
The developer overestimates the cost of switching paths because of sunk effort.
The developer silently substitutes a familiar tool.
The developer postpones the hard integration step after a demo succeeds.
Success is not celebrated, explained, or connected to the next action.
The next action is too large, ambiguous, or irreversible.
The developer cannot preserve or share progress.
AI and agent-mediated attempts fail
The coding agent chooses the wrong product, version, SDK, or tutorial.
The agent invents commands, APIs, configuration fields, limits, or features.
The agent follows stale training data instead of current docs.
The agent cannot access authenticated documentation or console state.
The agent lacks the organization, region, account, and permission context.
The agent exposes or mishandles secrets.
The agent performs a consequential action without a clear approval boundary.
The agent optimizes for a passing command instead of the developer's meaningful outcome.
The agent suppresses or works around a legitimate security or policy failure.
The agent changes multiple layers at once, obscuring the cause.
The agent leaves generated code, resources, or state the developer cannot explain.
The agent sees a false success signal and stops early.
The agent retries a non-idempotent action.
The agent cannot inspect UI-only status, email verification, MFA, or human approval.
The developer cannot review the volume or complexity of the agent's changes.
The platform blocks automation, browser control, headless login, or programmatic credentials.
Agent and human actions race or overwrite one another.
The developer cannot reproduce the agent's successful environment.
The agent cannot discover the canonical starting point or distinguish current documentation from stale, unofficial, or deprecated material.
Critical instructions are gated, client-rendered, or available only in images, video, or interactive UI that the agent cannot retrieve or interpret reliably.
A platform intended for programmatic use exposes no stable machine-readable interface or schema that the agent can inspect.
The machine-readable description, generated client, human documentation, and live platform behavior disagree.
Tool or operation names, descriptions, input schemas, constraints, errors, or side effects are too ambiguous for the agent to select and invoke safely.
Authentication supports an interactive human flow when the job requires an unattended machine identity, with no supported alternative.
Authentication supports a machine credential when the job requires explicit user delegation or consent, with no supported alternative.
Signup, CAPTCHA, MFA, terms acceptance, business verification, sales approval, or administrator consent cannot be handed to a human and then resumed by the agent.
The agent cannot discover authorization endpoints, required scopes, or a least-privilege path.
Bot controls, access policy, or terms make it unclear whether the intended automated use is permitted.
The agent cannot determine plan eligibility, remaining quota, price, credits, or the billable consequence before acting.
A consequential operation offers no supported preview, dry run, idempotency protection, confirmation boundary, receipt, or reversible recovery path for agent use.
Measurement and diagnosis produce a false story
Analytics miss developers who never load the instrumented surface.
Ad blockers, consent choices, network policy, or privacy tools suppress events.
One developer appears as several users across devices, accounts, CLI, and browser.
Several developers appear as one organization or shared account.
Bots, agents, demos, employees, and tests inflate completion.
A `200`, deploy status, generated key, or copied command is counted as meaningful success.
The platform records completion before the developer verifies the result.
The developer succeeds outside the expected path and appears to drop.
The developer fails after the tracked endpoint and appears successful.
Retry loops inflate step counts and apparent engagement.
Cohorts combine different intents, skills, regions, plans, and platform versions.
Survivorship bias hides people who could not start.
Support-assisted success is mixed with independent success.
Employee, partner, event, and customer traffic are mixed.
Correlation is treated as proof of the cause.
Room votes, survey answers, and stated preferences are treated as behavioral evidence.
The team fixes the most visible complaint instead of the load-bearing cause.
The metric creates pressure to shorten the milestone rather than improve the developer outcome.
Application deployment platform
The platform cannot identify the application's language, framework, runtime, or build system.
Automatic detection chooses the wrong runtime or version.
The repository root, service subdirectory, workspace, or monorepo package is misidentified.
The default build command is wrong.
The default start command is wrong.
The application listens on the wrong interface or port.
The platform injects a port that the application ignores.
The application builds locally but not in the platform's build environment.
The build requires private packages, submodules, Git LFS objects, or credentials that are unavailable.
A container image builds but has the wrong architecture, entrypoint, user, filesystem, or health check.
The service starts but exits because a required dependency or environment variable is absent.
The service is healthy internally but unreachable through routing, DNS, TLS, or firewall configuration.
The first request arrives before startup or migration completes.
The service sleeps, cold-starts, or scales to zero, so first access looks broken.
Logs show application output but hide build, routing, health-check, or control-plane failures.
A failed deploy leaves the prior version live, so the developer verifies the wrong code.
Preview, staging, and production environments use different variables, domains, or dependencies.
A database, cache, queue, persistent disk, worker, or scheduled job is required before the web service can work.
The platform provisions dependent resources in an order the application cannot tolerate.
A migration runs concurrently, twice, too late, or with insufficient privilege.
Ephemeral filesystem behavior destroys generated or uploaded state.
Build and runtime regions are confused with resource or user regions.
The deploy succeeds but the developer cannot find the public URL or distinguish it from an internal URL.
A custom domain or certificate is treated as necessary before the generated endpoint is verified.
Rollback, redeploy, restart, and rebuild have unclear or overlapping meanings.
Infrastructure primitive
The developer cannot choose among overlapping compute, storage, network, DNS, CDN, secret, or messaging products.
The console asks for capacity, topology, durability, or consistency choices before the developer has workload evidence.
A resource is provisioned in a network, subnet, project, account, or region the workload cannot reach.
Identity and access policy syntax is more complex than the core resource operation.
A resource policy and an identity policy disagree.
Public access is disabled when the tutorial assumes it, or enabled when policy forbids it.
DNS, routing, peering, NAT, gateway, firewall, security-group, or load-balancer dependencies are missing.
The resource has a control-plane identifier but no usable data-plane endpoint.
The SDK defaults to a different account or region than the console.
Eventual consistency delays policy, DNS, route, or resource visibility.
Capacity, instance type, accelerator, IP address, or zone is unavailable.
The primitive requires another primitive before it can be tested.
Encryption keys, certificates, or secrets must be provisioned first.
The developer can create the resource but cannot observe consumption or verify durability.
Cleanup has dependency order, retention, snapshot, or protection constraints.
The tutorial hides recurring cost, data transfer, reserved capacity, or minimum-use behavior.
The resource is correct but the client library uses incompatible protocol, addressing, or signature settings.
Local emulators differ materially from the managed service.
Infrastructure-as-code state disagrees with resources created in the console.
Organization policy, service control policy, admission control, or quota blocks the documented configuration.
Database and data infrastructure
The developer chooses the wrong database, storage model, index, consistency level, or deployment mode.
The connection string points to the wrong instance, database, branch, schema, replica, or region.
Network allowlists, private networking, TLS, certificates, or client verification block the connection.
The client driver, ORM, protocol, or wire version is incompatible.
Password encoding, certificate formatting, connection-string parsing, or secret injection corrupts credentials.
The database exists but the user lacks schema, table, query, index, or data privileges.
Schema creation, migration, or seed order is wrong.
The ORM generates unsupported SQL or data types.
Time zones, collations, encodings, nulls, numeric precision, or case sensitivity change results.
The developer writes to a primary but reads from a lagging replica.
Indexing, ingestion, compaction, replication, or vector embedding is asynchronous and invisible.
